coredata.dc:title: Plastic smoke aerosol: Nano-sized particle distribution, absorption/fluorescent properties, dysregulation of oxidative processes and synaptic transmission in rat brain nerve terminals
coredata.dc:creator: Borysov, Arsenii
Tarasenko, Alla
Krisanova, Natalia
Pozdnyakova, Natalia
Pastukhov, Artem
Dudarenko, Marina
Paliienko, Konstantin
Borisova, Tatiana
coredata.dc:description: Smoke from plastic waste incineration in an open air travels worldwide and is a major source of air pollution particulate matter (PM) that is very withstand to degradation and hazard to human health. Suspension of smoke aerosol components in water occurs during rains and fire extinguishing. Here, water-suspended plastic smoke aerosol (WPS) preparations suitable for biotesting were synthesized. It has been revealed using dynamic light scattering that WPS contained major nano-sized (∼30 nm) PM fraction, and this result was confirmed by electron microscopy. Optical absorption of WPS was in the UV region and an increase in λex led to a red-shift in fluorescence emission with a corresponding decrease in fluorescence intensity. WPS was analyzed in neurotoxicity studies in vitro using presynaptic rat cortex nerve terminals (synaptosomes). Generation of spontaneous reactive oxygen species (ROS) detected using fluorescent dye 2′,7-dichlorofluorescein in nerve terminals was decreased by WPS (10–50 μg/ml) in a dose-dependent manner. WPS also reduced the H2O2-evoked ROS production in synaptosomes, thereby influencing cellular oxidative processes and this effect was similar to that for carbon nanodots. WPS (0.1 mg/ml) decreased the synaptosomal membrane potential and synaptic vesicle acidification in fluorimetric experiments. WPS (1.0 mg/ml) attenuated the synaptosomal transporter-mediated uptake of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters, L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA, respectively. This can lead to an excessive increase in the glutamate concentration in the synaptic cleft and neurotoxicity via over activation of ionotropic glutamate receptors. Therefore, WPS was neurotoxic and provoked presynaptic malfunction through changes of oxidative activity, reduction of the membrane potential, synaptic vesicle acidification, and transporter-mediated uptake of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters in nerve terminals. In summary, synthesis and emission to the environment of ultrafine PM occur during combustion of plastics, thereby polluting air and water resources, and possibly triggering development of neuropathologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Dynamic light scattering histograms of WPS preparations: particle number distribution. Five measurements are present in the histograms, each during 1 min, sequentially 1 − red, 2 − blue, 3 − green, 4 − grey, and 5 − purple. b) Transmission electron microscopy images of WPS samples (insert: PM image magnification). Scale bar −100 nm.

WPS-induced changes in the membrane potential of nerve terminals. WPS (0.025, 0.05 and 0.1 mg/ml) and KCl (30 mM) were added to nerve terminals preliminary equilibrated with 0.5 μM rhodamine 6G; inset) – WPS-induced responses at higher magnification. All kinetic traces are presented after accounting for dilution. Each trace is representative of six experimental data records performed with different synaptosomal preparations.

WPS-induced changes in synaptic vesicle acidification. a) WPS at concentrations of 0.05 and 0.1 mg/ml was added to nerve terminals pre-equilibrated with 5 μM acridine orange; inset ‒ WPS-induced responses at higher magnification after accounting for dilution; b) the kinetics of acridine orange accumulation by synaptic vesicles of nerve terminals pre-incubated with WPS (0.1 and 0.4 mg/ml). Each trace is representative of six experimental data records performed with different synaptosomal preparations.

WPS-induced changes in spontaneous (a) and H2O2-evoked (b) ROS generation in nerve terminals. Each trace is representative of six experimental data records performed with different synaptosomal preparations.

A decrease in the initial rate of synaptosomal transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate (a) and [3H]GABA (b) in the presence of WPS. Data is mean ± SEM. ∗∗, p < 0.01; ∗∗∗, p < 0.001 as compared to the control, n = 6.

head.ce:title: Plastic smoke aerosol: Nano-sized particle distribution, absorption/fluorescent properties, dysregulation of oxidative processes and synaptic transmission in rat brain nerve terminals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Department of Neurochemistry, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine, 9 Leontovicha str, Kiev, 01030, Ukraine
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Department of Neurochemistry, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine, 9 Leontovicha str, Kiev, 01030, Ukraine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Neurochemistry, Palladin Institute of Biochemistry, NAS of Ukraine, 9 Leontovicha Street, Kiev, 01030, Ukraine.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Smoke from plastic waste incineration in an open air travels worldwide and is a major source of air pollution particulate matter (PM) that is very withstand to degradation and hazard to human health. Suspension of smoke aerosol components in water occurs during rains and fire extinguishing. Here, water-suspended plastic smoke aerosol (WPS) preparations suitable for biotesting were synthesized. It has been revealed using dynamic light scattering that WPS contained major nano-sized (∼30 nm) PM fraction, and this result was confirmed by electron microscopy. Optical absorption of WPS was in the UV region and an increase in λex led to a red-shift in fluorescence emission with a corresponding decrease in fluorescence intensity. WPS was analyzed in neurotoxicity studies in vitro using presynaptic rat cortex nerve terminals (synaptosomes). Generation of spontaneous reactive oxygen species (ROS) detected using fluorescent dye 2′,7-dichlorofluorescein in nerve terminals was decreased by WPS (10–50 μg/ml) in a dose-dependent manner. WPS also reduced the H2O2-evoked ROS production in synaptosomes, thereby influencing cellular oxidative processes and this effect was similar to that for carbon nanodots. WPS (0.1 mg/ml) decreased the synaptosomal membrane potential and synaptic vesicle acidification in fluorimetric experiments. WPS (1.0 mg/ml) attenuated the synaptosomal transporter-mediated uptake of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters, L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA, respectively. This can lead to an excessive increase in the glutamate concentration in the synaptic cleft and neurotoxicity via over activation of ionotropic glutamate receptors. Therefore, WPS was neurotoxic and provoked presynaptic malfunction through changes of oxidative activity, reduction of the membrane potential, synaptic vesicle acidification, and transporter-mediated uptake of excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters in nerve terminals. In summary, synthesis and emission to the environment of ultrafine PM occur during combustion of plastics, thereby polluting air and water resources, and possibly triggering development of neuropathologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, plastic manufacture is intended to be reduced due to environmental debates worldwide on its dangers and hazards (Thompson et al., 2009; Chae and An, 2018; Prata et al., 2019). A large part of plastics is still utilised being subjected to intentional or incidental open-fire burning notwithstanding increasing techniques for its recycling. Plastics are easily combustible and open burning of plastic waste or even stove heating with it is a widespread practice of utilization in low- and middle-income countries. Smoke contains permanent gases, volatile organic compounds and particulate matter (PM) (Statheropoulos and Karma, 2007). PM of size less than 2.5 μm and 10 μm represents environmental monitoring standards PM2.5 and PM10, respectively. Composition of plastic smoke was examined (Simoneit et al., 2005; Verma et al., 2016), and 195 compounds were identified in the low-density polyethylene smoke emissions (Hosseini et al., 2014). Plastic combustion products become incorporated into the environment and are a major source of air pollution. Air pollution PM disperses worldwide and travels across regions, state boundaries and continents (Jiang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2017). The main origin of black carbon is wildfires, biomass burning and fuel exhaust and this organic matter is very withstand to degradation (Tranvik, 2018; Long et al., 2013; Landrigan et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2014). Carbon-containing particles from aerosol owning highly adhesive surface can bind different surrounding molecules that in turn makes their surface oxidized and charged. Suspension of smoke aerosol components in water occurs during rains, fire control and extinguishing. When carbon-containing particles become water soluble they can contaminate water resources, including sea and ocean (Tranvik, 2018).

The emission of PM2.5 into the atmosphere establishes serious problem to human health and amount of death associated with air pollution is growing (Hata et al., 2014; Lelieveld et al., 2015; Pope et al., 2011; Luo et al., 2019). Air pollution with PM2.5 is linked to elevated cardiovascular and pulmonary diseases, increased rates of asthma attacks, lung cancer and premature death (Kelly and Fussell, 2015; Valavandis et al., 2008). Also, PM2.5 air pollution is causally associated with nervous system disorders and neurodegenerative disease, including redaction of cognitive function, attention deficit/hyperactivity in children, dementia in adults, autism, and stroke (Landrigan et al., 2018).

In particular, emission components of burning plastic increase risk of heart diseases, worsen respiratory ones (asthma and emphysema), and cause nervous system damages, nausea and headaches (Verma et al., 2016). Microplastics cause neurotoxicity and oxidative damage in the European seabass (Barboza et al., 2018).

The World Health Organisation (2006) noted that PM2.5 average concentrations should not exceed 10 μg/m3 annually and 25 μg/m3 daily, but these thresholds are exceeded in many of the world’s cities (Guerreiro et al., 2014; He et al., 2017; Vande Hey et al., 2018). Importantly, ultrafine (nano-sized) air pollution PM0.1 (size less than 0.1 μm) is more hazardous to human health as compared to fine PM2.5 and coarse PM10, nevertheless, ultrafine PM is not regularly monitored (Fine et al., 2004; Nixdorff et al., 2018).

The central nervous system of humans is not secured by the blood brain barrier against evolutionary new challenge of intensive exposure to anthropogenic air pollution PM (Borisova, 2018). Micro- and nano-sized particles can be deposited in the nasal region, and then can move along olfactory nerve axons directly to the brain circumstancing the blood brain barrier (Oberdörster et al., 2005).

The main excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters in the central nervous system are glutamate and γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA), respectively. Disturbance of their transport characteristics contributes to the pathogenesis of main neurological and neurodegenerative disorders and diseases (Borisova and Borysov, 2016; Borisova, 2016). Carbon-containing nanoparticles obtained by combustion of organic compounds considerably dysregulated presynaptic processes, in particular they affected Na+-dependent uptake and ambient level of glutamate and GABA, exocytotic release, membrane potential in brain nerve terminals and acidification of synaptic vesicles (Borisova, 2019; Borisova, 2014, Borisova et al., 2015; 2017; Nixdorff et al., 2018).

The aims of this study were: 1) to develop and synthesize w ater-suspended p lastic s moke aerosol (WPS) preparations appropriate for further biological experiments; 2) to assess the distribution and the average size of PM in WPS using dynamic light scattering; 3) to analyse the collected samples using electron microscopy and define their spectral characteristics; 4) to examine WPS influence on key presynaptic processes and characteristics using nerve terminals (synaptosomes) isolated from rat cortex. In particular, effects of WPS on (i) the membrane potential; (ii) synaptic vesicle acidification; (iii) spontaneous generation of endogenous reactive oxygen species (ROS) and hydrogen peroxide-induced ROS production; (iiii) the initial rate of Na+-dependent uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA; and (iiiii) hydrogen peroxide-induced release of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA were investigated in nerve terminals.

EDTA, HEPES, sucrose, D-glucose, Ficoll 400, the fluorescent dye 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein and analytical grade salts were obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA); aqueous counting scintillator, organic counting scintillator were purchased from Sigma (USA) and Amersham (Little Chalfont, UK); L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA (γ-[2,3-3H(N)]-aminobutyric acid) were from PerkinElmer (Waltham, MA, USA); the fluorescent dyes acridine orange and rhodamine 6G were obtained from Molecular Probes (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and synthesis of WPS preparations suitable for biological experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Smoke aerosol emission of plastics (pieces of polyethylene terephthalate bottles) was quantitatively collected in laboratory conditions during mixed combustion phase (when the flaming and smoldering phases were present at the same time) and smoldering phase (Hosseini et al., 2014), and then bubbled through water according to the procedure recently developed in our laboratory for wood smoke aerosol (Shelestov et al., 2019). The presence of PM in smoke emission samples was monitored in real time using air quality monitor/mini laser PM2.5 monitor/SDL607. Obtained WPS samples were dried to measure the concentrations of smoke components, and also WPS samples were calibrated and standardised by their optical characteristics for further biological experiments.

The above WPS samples were not completely applicable for biotesting using tissue preparations and optical experiments because of their significant heterogeneity, instability, continuous aggregation and sedimentation of large particulate components. Taking into account the fact that the most hazardous to human health is nano-sized air pollution PM (Borisova, 2018), WPS samples enriched with nano-sized PM were prepared using simple approach, where the smoke samples were filtered through Millipore filters (0.45 μm) before starting optical and biological experiments (Supplementary Material, Subsection 1, Fig. 1 ).

The size of particles in WPS preparations has been monitored according to (Shelestov et al., 2019) by dynamic light scattering using a laser correlation spectrometer Zetasizer-3, Malvern Instruments (UK) equipped with He–Ne laser LGN-111 (P = 25 mW, k = 633 nm).
Intact plastic smoke PM and WPS samples were examined and photographed using a transmission electron microscope (H-600, Hitachi, Japan).

WPS was analyzed using FTIR spectrometer INVENIO-R (Bruker, Germany). Micro-Raman measurements were performed using a triple Raman spectrometer T-64000 Horiba Jobin-Yvon, equipped with electrically cooled charge-coupled device detector and Olympus BX41 microscope (for details, see Supplementary Material, Subsection 3).

Wistar rats, males, body weight of 100–120 g, were kept in a temperature-controlled (22–23 °C) room using Institutional facilities, and provided with water and dry food ad libitum. All animal experiments were conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki, “Scientific Requirements and Research Protocols” and “Research Ethics Committees”, and the ARRIVE guidelines (McGrath et al., 2010). The experimental protocols from January 11, 2018 were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of the Institute. In particular, 12 rats were used for analysis of glutamate and GABA uptake and 6 rats − for fluorescence measurements.

Synaptosomes are useful preparations to study presynaptic processes (Sudhof, 2004). Synaptosomes were isolated from cortex homogenates by differential and density gradient centrifugation with Ficoll-400 according to (Cotman, 1974) with insignificant improvements (Borisova, 2013; Pozdnyakova et al., 2016). Concentration of proteins was monitored according to (Larson et al., 1986).

WPS aliquots were added to synaptosomal suspensions and incubated for 10 min. Then, mixture of 10 μM glutamate and L-[14C]glutamate (420 nM, 0.1 μCi/ml) was added to synaptosomal suspensions to initiate uptake and further incubated at 37 °C for 1,2 and 10 min, and after that synaptosomes were sedimented at 10,000 g for 20 s. Uptake of L-[14C]glutamate by synaptosomes was measured as described in (Borisova et al., 2016; Pastukhov and Borisova, 2018a, 2018b).

WPS aliquots were added to synaptosomal suspensions and incubated for 10 min. Synaptosomal uptake was initiated by the addition of 1 μM GABA and [3H]GABA (50 nM, 4.7 μCi/ml). [3H]GABA uptake was terminated in different time intervals (1, 3, and 5 min) by filtering aliquots through a Whatman GF/C filters and recorded according to (Krisanova et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The plasma membrane potential (E m) of nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: The plasma membrane potential of synaptosomes was monitored with a potentiometric fluorescent dye rhodamine 6G using a fluorescence spectrophotometer QuantaMasterTM 40 (PTI, Inc., Canada) as described in (Borysov et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synaptic vesicle acidification in nerve terminals
ce:section.ce:para: Acidification of synaptic vesicles was analyzed with a pH-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange using a fluorescence spectrofluorometer QuantaMaster™ 40 (PTI Inc., Canada) according to (Tarasenko et al., 2010). To study the effects of high doses of WPS and to avoid large sample dilution, synaptosomes were pre-incubated in the standard salt solution containing up to 0.4 mg/ml WPS for 5 min before acridine orange addition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) detection with 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF)
ce:section.ce:para: A cell-permeable non-fluorescent probe, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydro-fluoresceindiacetate (H2-DCFDA) was used to measure ROS production in synaptosomes. The probe becomes highly fluorescent upon oxidation after intracellular de-esterification. Synaptosomes (0.2 mg of protein/ml) were incubated with H2-DCFDA (5 μM) for 5 min in stirred thermostatted cuvette at 37 °C, and after that the kinetic measurements were fulfilled. DCF fluorescence changes were recorded at excitation and emission wavelengths of 502 and 525 nm, respectively (slit bands were 2 nm each) using a fluorescence spectrofluorometer QuantaMaster™ 40 (PTI, Inc., Canada).
Experimental data were expressed as mean ± S.E.M. (n independent experiments). The differences between two groups were compared by One-way ANOVA. Differences were significant, when p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: WPS analysis by dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of particles in WPS preparations was proven measuring particle distribution by intensity, volume and number with dynamic light scattering. Peak analysis (intensity) revealed that the overall mean was 880 ± 80 nm (Supplementary Material, Subsection 1, Fig. 1a). Peak analysis (volume) showed heterogeneity of particles in WPS samples, and two main particle populations were found, the average size of which was 55 ± 9 nm and 1215 ± 200 nm (Supplementary Material, Subsection 1, Fig. 1b). Particle number distribution showed that the major fraction of particles in WPS preparations was 31 ± 3 nm, and so they can be classified as ultrafine PM < 0.1 (Fig. 1).

The main optical absorption of WPS was observed in the UV region, mostly within the range of 250–320 nm. An increase in λex led to a red-shift in the fluorescence emission with a corresponding decrease in the fluorescence intensity (Supplementary Material, Subsection 4, Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments with nerve terminals using potential-, pH-, and ROS-sensitive fluorescent dyes
The membrane potential of nerve terminals in the presence of WPS
ce:section.ce:para: The membrane potential of nerve terminals and acidification of synaptic vesicles are tightly coupled with neurotransmitter accumulation. Effect of WPS on the plasma membrane potential of nerve terminals was monitored with the cationic potentiometric fluorescent dye rhodamine 6G. WPS per se did not change the emission spectrum of rhodamine 6G (data not shown), despite its own photoluminescence. As Fig. 2 shows, the addition of rhodamine 6G (0.5 μM) to synaptosomal suspension was accompanied by partial quenching of dye fluorescence due to its binding to negatively charged sides of synaptosomal membranes. Further addition of WPS to synaptosomes at the concentrations of 0.025, 0.05 and 0.1 mg/ml led to an increase in the dye fluorescence indicating membrane depolarization. This depolarization was much lesser as compared to high K+ (30 mM) -induced one, but it was dose-dependent, as can be more clearly seen at higher magnification (Fig. 2, inset).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synaptic vesicle acidification in the presence of WPS
ce:section.ce:para: A рН-sensitive fluorescent dye acridine orange, accumulated by the acidic lumina of synaptic vesicles, was used to study influence of WPS on synaptic vesicle acidification. WPS per se did not influence the emission spectra of acridine orange (data not shown). As it was shown in Fig. 3a, the application of acridine orange to synaptosomes resulted in partial quenching of dye fluorescence due to its accumulation in synaptic vesicles. The subsequent addition of 0.05 and 0.1 mg/ml WPS led to an insignificant increase in acridine orange fluorescence, which nevertheless was dose-dependent, as can be seen at higher magnification (Fig. 3a, inset). Fig. 3b demonstrated that the more doses of WPS in synaptosomal preparations, the less pronounced a dye quenching indicating a reduced accumulation of acridine orange by synaptic vesicles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spontaneous and hydrogen peroxide-evoked ROS generation in nerve terminals in the presence of WPS
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate weather WPS influenced redox state of nerve terminals, the kinetics of ROS generation was monitored using fluorescent dye 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2-DCFDA). Fig. 4a shows spontaneous ROS generation in synaptosomal suspension in control and in the presence of WPS (10 and 50 μg/ml). Spontaneous ROS production was attenuated by WPS in a dose-dependent manner that was demonstrated by decreased intensity of DCF fluorescence. Influence of WPS became much more pronounced after the addition of 0.1 mM H2O2 to synaptosomes (Fig. 4b).

It was suggested that these effects could be caused by particulate carbon component of WPS. To address this assumption, carbon nanodots synthesized from β-alanine (Borisova et al., 2015) were used in the experiments. It was revealed that carbon nanodots exerted similar effects on spontaneous and H2O2-evoked ROS generation in nerve terminals (Supplementary Material, Subsection 5, Fig. 5 ). Hydrogen peroxide-induced changes in the membrane potential of nerve terminals and synaptic vesicle acidification in the presence of WPS can be found in Supplementary Material, Subsection 6, Fig. 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiments with nerve terminals using radiolabeled neurotransmitter assay
The initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate by nerve terminals in the presence of WPS
ce:section.ce:para: The initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA, respectively, were assessed in nerve terminals to address the question how WPS-induced changes in the membrane potential, synaptic vesicle acidification and ROS production influenced neurotransmitter transport characteristics. The addition of WPS to synaptosomes caused a dose-dependent decrease in the initial rate of L-[14C]glutamate uptake. As shown in Fig. 5a, it was equal to 2.25 ± 0.14 nmol/min/mg of protein in the control and 1.89 ± 0.11 nmol/min/mg of protein in the presence of 0.2 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 4.91; p = 0,51]; and 1.05 ± 0.20 nmol/min/mg of protein in the presence of 1.0 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 29.74; p < 0.001]. Accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate by synaptosomes for 10 min consisted of 11.52 ± 0.35 nmol/mg of protein in the control and 10.32 ± 0.39 nmol/mg of protein in the presence of 0.2 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 4.2; p = 0,68] and 6.52 ± 0.40 nmol/mg of protein in the presence of 1.0 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 79.06; p < 0.001]. Therefore, WPS inhibited the initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate by synaptosomes, and these parameters decreased by 19% and 11%, respectively, in the presence of WPS at a low concentration (0.2 mg/ml), and by 44% and 37%, respectively, at a high WPS concentration (1.0 mg/ml).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of [3H]GABA by nerve terminals in the presence of WPS
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5b, the initial rate of transporter-mediated [3H]GABA uptake by synaptosomes was equal to 163.28 ± 9.10 pmol/min/mg of protein in the control and 163.23 ± 10.83 pmol/min/mg of protein in the presence of 0.2 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 0,000017; p = 0,99]; and 100.51 ± 6.77 pmol/min/mg of protein in the presence of 1.0 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 19.79; p < 0.01]. Accumulation of [3H]GABA by synaptosomes for 5 min consisted of 538.71 ± 24.39 pmol/mg of protein in the control and 518.08 ± 27.96 pmol/mg of protein in the presence of 0.2 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 4.02; p = 0.072] and 273.48 ± 28.90 pmol/mg of protein in the presence of 1.0 mg/ml WPS [F(1,10) = 49.19; p < 0.001]. Therefore, WPS inhibited the initial rate of transporter-mediated uptake and accumulation of [3H]GABA by synaptosomes, whereas only [3H]GABA accumulation decreased by 11% in the presence of WPS at a low concentration (0.2 mg/ml), and both parameters decreased by 39% and 50%, respectively, at high WPS concentration (1.0 mg/ml). In summary, WPS attenuated synaptosomal transporter-mediated uptake of both excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmitters.
Hydrogen peroxide-evoked release of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA from nerve terminals in the presence of WPS were also analyzed (Supplementary Material, Subsection 7).

Dynamic light scattering showed that WPS contained not only micro-sized particles, but also nano-sized ones, and the major number of particles in WPS had the average diameter of 31 ± 3 nm (Fig. 1). Electron microscopy confirmed the existence and size distribution of smoke aerosol particles, and demonstrated structural heterogeneity of smoke PM that contained black inclusions (Fig. 1b, and Supplementary Material, Fig. 2a and b). The presence of nano-sized particles is not a characteristic feature of plastic smoke aerosols because the major number of particles in previously developed preparations of water-suspended wood smoke aerosols was also about 30 nm (Shelestov et al., 2019). The funding regarding majority of nano-scale particles in WPS is of value for environmental monitoring and air quality assessment algorithms because plastic waste burning under open-fire conditions is a major source of air pollution that disperses globally and travels across state borders.

Besides ultrafine PM of plastic origin, large numbers of nano-sized particles smaller than 100 nm pollute typical ambient air with a level ranging between 5000 and 10,000 particles per ml that increases up to 3,000,000 particles per ml during pollution episodes (Donaldson and Stone, 2003). The concentration of ultrafine particles varies from region to region and from season to season. Nano-sized particles make up about 70% of total ambient aerosols in urban areas, while their mass contribution is only about 1% (Buzea et al., 2007; Tuch et al., 1997). Other studies have indicated that the size distribution of a typical ultrafine aerosol has a mode diameter within the range of 20–40 nm, and over 90–99% of particles are below 100 nm in size (Fine et al., 2004). Many observations of nucleation events showed the presence of particles even below 10 nm in diameter (Fine et al., 2004), for example, in urban Atlanta, most particles were below 10 nm, but others were around 20–30 nm in size (Fine et al., 2004; Woo et al., 2001). A significant amount of solid particles with a median diameter approximately 75 nm was observed in engine exhaust, during biomass fuel combustion, candle burning, cooking activities, e.g. grilling, and it was generally consistent with combustion/pyrolysis processes of organic materials (Lee and Bae, 2016; Pratte et al., 2017). More than 30% of the smoke particles from the burning of biomass fuel (rubber wood, whole wood pellets and rice husks) were less than 100 nm (Hata et al., 2014). No meaningful correlation between particle number and mass was shown, thereby indicating that fine particle standards in environmental monitoring may not be effective in controlling ultrafine particle concentrations with mode diameters from 30 nm up to 100 nm (Fine et al., 2004).

Literature data emphasize that the lesser the size of nanoparticle is, the more health hazard they can cause (Borisova, 2018). Particle toxicity has been revealed to be higher for smaller in size particles on a per unit mass basis (Li et al., 2002; Oberdörster and Utell, 2002; Peters et al., 1997; Fine et al., 2004). Airborne PM has several mechanisms of cellular effects, such as cytotoxicity through oxidative stress, oxygen-free radical-generating activity, mutagenicity, DNA oxidative damage, and stimulation of proinflammatory factors. The smaller the size of PM, the higher toxicity through mechanisms of oxidative stress and inflammation (Valavandis et al., 2008).

Light absorption and fluorescent properties of WPS were characterized in details (Supplementary Material, Subsection 4 and 5, Figs. 4 and 5). Comparison of fluorescent characteristics of carbon nanodots (Hu et al., 2014) and WPS showed the similarity, thereby proving that carbon-containing particles were components of WPS, which determined at least partially its fluorescent properties. This finding coincided with literature data because it was demonstrated using the X-ray analyses that solid particles of engine exhaust, biomass fuel combustion, candle burning, and cooking activities were composed mainly of carbon-based materials and oxygen (Lee and Bae, 2016). Amorphous carbon PM with diameter smaller than 100 nm was found in cigarette smoke (Fariss et al., 2013; Pratte et al., 2017). Plastic smoke consisted of black carbon and a lot of organic compounds (Simoneit et al., 2005; Verma et al., 2016).

Specific light absorption and fluorescent properties of WPS can be used in environment monitoring technologies for “smart” air quality assessment subdividing lesser toxic air pollution PM, e.g. natural mineral dust, and more toxic plastic smoke PM. Also, correspondence of optical density of WPS preparations and weight of dried plastic smoke components (Supplementary Material, Subsection 4, Fig. 4e) can be applicable not only in laboratory experiments, but also in air quality assessment during incidents of open-fire burning of plastics.

WPS was analyzed in neurotoxicity studies in vitro using presynaptic nerve terminals and parameters included in the Guidelines for Neurotoxicity Risk Assessment of US EPA, 1998 (3.1.2. Animal Studies; 3.1.2.3. Neurochemical Endpoints of Neurotoxicity; 3.1.3.4. In Vitro Data in Neurotoxicology) and WHO. WPS during acute application to nerve terminals affected key processes involved in synaptic neurotransmission, thereby causing presynaptic malfunctioning. In particular, WPS (1.0 mg/ml) caused depolarization of the plasma membrane of nerve terminals (Fig. 2), affected acidification of synaptic vesicles (Fig. 3), reduced the initial rate of uptake and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA (Fig. 5). Capability of WPS to depolarize the plasma membrane indicated that plastic smoke components can disturb the integrity of biological membranes, and so activity of membrane-associated proteins. In turn, this can lower the initial rate and accumulation of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA by nerve terminals. Also, alterations in synaptic vesicle acidification can significantly influence uptake efficiency. A decrease in glutamate uptake can have rigorous consequences increasing the ambient glutamate level in the synaptic cleft, thereby causing neurotoxicity through over activation of post synaptic ionotropic glutamate receptors. WPS-induced weakening of glutamate and GABA transporter activity can cause severe neurological outcome via disturbance of excitation/inhibition balance in the central nervous system.

Data regarding antioxidant capability of WPS (Fig. 4, and Supplementary Material, Subsection 5, Fig. 5) and carbon-containing nanoparticles (Supplementary Material, Subsection 5, Fig. 5) are in agreement with the literature data. It has been demonstrated recently that carbon nanodots own antioxidant properties by scavenging oxidant free radicals and reactive oxygen species (Zhang et al., 2018; Ji et al., 2019), and the carboxyl and amino groups contribute to this capability through hydrogen atom transfer reaction (Ji et al., 2019). Also, antioxidant activity of plastic organics was demonstrated (Simoneit et al., 2005). Capability of WPS to decrease spontaneous and H2O2-induced ROS generation demonstrated that WPS can imbalance oxidative processes in nerve terminals (Fig. 4). WPS caused a dose-dependent reduction of H2O2-induced membrane depolarization in synaptosomes and H2O2-evoked dissipation of synaptic vesicle proton gradient (Supplementary Material, Subsection 6, Fig. 6). However, WPS cannot mitigate H2O2-stimulated release of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA from nerve terminals (Supplementary Material, Subsection 7). In this context, it can be concluded that a WPS-mediated decrease in H2O2-evoked membrane depolarization and synaptic vesicle acidification was not sufficient to mitigate H2O2-evoked release of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA from nerve terminals. WSP-induced antioxidant activity in nerve terminals did not prevent excitotoxic changes in glutamate transporter functioning (Fig. 5). Importantly, different types of ROS influence glutamate homeostasis in their own ways, and different antioxidants have diverse protection effects against glutamate-induced cell death in cell lines (Mazzio et al., 2001). Notable, alterations in oxidative processes can affect normal synaptic neurotransmission, because our previous study has revealed a tight correlation between ROS production and GABA secretion in nerve terminals (Tarasenko et al., 2012).

The experimental data on dose-dependency of WPS effects on the neurotransmitter transport characteristics allowed establishing a minimal toxic concentration of plastic smoke PM, even a local excess of which in the biological fluids and tissues can lead to harmful heath consequences. These results should be further advanced in order to use them in environmental monitoring of incidents of plastic burning. Neurochemical data are also in accordance with our recent results on carbon nanodots synthesized from β-alanine (Borisova et al., 2015) and thiourea (Borisova et al., 2017), where these nanoparticles decreased the plasma membrane potential, synaptic vesicle acidification and uptake of L-[14C]glutamate and [3H]GABA in nerve terminals.

In summary, synthesized WPS contained the main nano-sized PM fraction. Due to nano-scale size, smoke aerosol PM possesses unique characteristics inherent to nanoparticles and their environmental distribution and health effects can significantly differ from those of micro-sized PM. Light absorption and fluorescent properties of WPS were characterized in details and their specificity can be further advanced in environmental monitoring for sensing of smoke plastic particles. WPS demonstrated neurotoxic features and provoked presynaptic malfunction via weakening of transporter-mediated uptake of glutamate and GABA in nerve terminals that misbalanced excitatory and inhibitory neurotransmission. WPS attenuated the membrane potential and synaptic vesicle acidification, and influenced oxidative processes in nerve terminals. Therefore, synthesis and emission of ultrafine PM to the environment occur during plastic burning, thereby polluting air and water resources, and being a possible trigger factor for the development of neuropathologies.
